# The shape of the course {#sec-course-shape}

The workshop is four hours in the room, but the learner journey is longer. It starts with pre-course work done at home and ends with a wrap-up that points forward. Understanding this shape tells you where to put your effort.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    subgraph before[Before the day]
        PW[Pre-course work<br/>accounts, installs, first commit]
    end
    subgraph day[The four hours]
        I[Intro and motivation] --> H1[Hands-on: create and clone]
        H1 --> H2[Hands-on: branch]
        H2 --> H3[Hands-on: pull request and merge]
        H3 --> H4[Hands-on: publish]
        H4 --> W[Wrap-up]
    end
    subgraph after[After the day]
        F[Follow-up workshop<br/>agentic coding]
    end
    before --> day --> after
```

## Pre-course work carries the setup

The single biggest risk to a hands-on workshop is spending the first ninety minutes installing software. So none of that happens in the room. Every account and install is pre-course work, done at home, days before, with a verification step that proves it worked.

The pre-work is a short, numbered sequence the learner follows on their own: create a GitHub account, install R and RStudio, install Git, generate a personal access token, join the chat channel, make a first commit to a test repository, and do one short reading. @sec-learner-setup turns this into the exact checklist you hand out.

The verification step is the point. A learner whose first commit landed before the workshop arrives ready to learn. A learner who skipped the pre-work is a support case you handle during the break (TODO: this cannot happen during the break, but needs to be addressed via direct communication with the learner prior to the workshop), not during the teaching.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**New educator** — protect the pre-work deadline. Send it with enough lead time. The pre-course survey (@sec-before) is partly there to tell you who has not finished.
:::

## The four hours are mostly hands-on

The in-room time is a tight loop: a short concept, then a "your turn" task where everyone does it, then a regroup. The slides exist to frame each loop, not to fill time. The real content is the learners' own hands on their own keyboards.

TODO: Don't use the term "show". 

@sec-run-of-show breaks the four hours into a timed run of show. The structure is always the same: motivate, demonstrate once, hand it over, regroup.

## The side benefits are real

The headline outcome is Git and GitHub for collaboration. But because the workshop runs inside R, RStudio, and real project files, learners absorb three things additionally:

- **More fluency in R and RStudio.** They use the interface for real work, not toy examples, and grow more comfortable with it.

- **A first taste of Quarto.** The material they touch is authored in Quarto. They see how prose, code, and output live in one document, which plants a seed for reproducible reporting.

- **A first step onto the terminal.** Earlier versions stayed entirely in the RStudio interface. TODO: This version does not yet have the terminal work. It will be build into the next iteration, based on which this guide will be updated. Revise:

This version introduces a small amount of terminal work, so learners leave less afraid of the command line.

Name these benefits in the wrap-up. Learners came for Git, and it helps them to notice they also moved forward on three other fronts.

## The wrap-up points forward

The last block is not a summary slide. It is a short reflection on what they can now do, an explicit naming of the side benefits above, and a pointer to the follow-up workshop on agentic coding. The goal is momentum: they should leave wanting to use this on their own work tomorrow.
